prompt:
I own the control-task layer of our YAML workflow DSL and I need it rebuilt as forwait.ts in this repo. The data-flow/interpolation layer is a different module owned by another team — this one is ONLY about control: `for:` loops with nested do-blocks, `wait:` durations against an injected logical clock, and `then: end`, plus just enough set/call for loop bodies to do something. Plain synchronous TypeScript that runs directly under `node --test` (type stripping — so no enums/namespaces, and keep the explicit ./forwait.ts extension in mind for anything you import).

Housekeeping first: fresh box, nothing installed. YAML parsing MUST use the pinned `yaml` package from package.json — install exactly what package-lock.json says (npm ci) into the project's own node_modules. `bash run_verify.sh` is what CI runs — it refuses to start without node_modules and then runs the node test runner. Don't touch run_verify.sh, test_forwait.ts, package.json, or package-lock.json.

API: export `runWorkflow(source, options)` where source is YAML text and options is `{ input?, handlers?, clock? }` (all optional; input defaults to {}, handlers to {}). Returns `{ status: 'completed', context }` — errors are thrown, not returned. Export `LoadError` (extends Error) for document validation. Handlers are `name -> (args) => result`. The clock is any object with `advance(ms)`; the engine never sleeps and never reads wall time.

Document shape + load validation (all LoadError, checked before anything executes): `document:` with dsl exactly "1.0" plus non-empty namespace and name; `do:` a non-empty ordered list of single-key maps (key = task name, value = body); task names unique within their own do block; the name "input" is reserved for the workflow input and rejected. Each task body has exactly one type key among `set:` / `call:` / `for:` / `wait:`, plus optionally `with:` (call only) and `then:`. `then:` may only be "continue" or "end" — this engine has no jump targets, anything else is a LoadError. A `for:` needs `each:` (loop variable name) and `in:` (expression), optionally `at:` (index variable name), and a non-empty nested `do:` list validated by the same rules. A `wait:` value is an ISO-8601 duration validated AT LOAD TIME: `P[nD][T[nH][nM][nS]]` with non-negative integers only — at least one component, no fractions, no lone "PT", no spaces, nothing else (so PT2S, PT1M30S, P1DT2H3M4S are fine; "PT", "5m", "PT1.5S" are LoadErrors). Convert to milliseconds when you parse.

Expressions: a string that is EXACTLY `${ <path> }` and nothing else evaluates to the raw typed value; every other string is a literal (mixed-text splicing belongs to the data-flow layer, not here). `${ . }` is the whole current view. A path is a leading dot then `.ident` segments and `[n]` non-negative integer indexes, e.g. `${ .scan[1].ping.n }`. Resolution walks the SCOPE CHAIN for the first segment: innermost loop scope outward to the workflow context, first scope that HAS the key wins (so a loop variable shadows a context key of the same name inside the body, and the context key is untouched afterwards). Missing anywhere yields undefined — never an error. `set:` bodies and `with:` args are evaluated recursively through nested objects/arrays; non-string scalars pass through.

Execution model: context starts as `{ input: <options.input> }`. Top-level tasks run strictly in list order and each records its result in the context under the task name — except `wait:`, which records nothing.
- `set:` result = its evaluated map. `call: <name>`: evaluate `with:` (default {}), invoke handlers[name], result = the return value; an unregistered name is an error whose message includes the name; handler exceptions propagate untouched.
- `wait:`: call `options.clock.advance(ms)` with the parsed duration. Executing a wait with no clock configured is an error whose message mentions the clock. A workflow that never executes a wait must run fine without one.
- `for:`: evaluate `in:` against the current scope chain; the value MUST be an array — anything else is a TypeError. Iterate in array order. Each iteration gets a fresh child scope holding the `each` variable, the `at` index variable if declared, and the results of the body's tasks as they complete (so later body tasks can read earlier ones' results by name, same as top level). The loop task's recorded result is an ARRAY of per-iteration records, each record an object mapping body task names to their results — nested loops nest their own arrays inside those records. An empty collection records an empty array. Loop variables and body-task results are NOT visible outside the loop; the per-iteration records under the loop task's name are the only thing that escapes.
- `then: end` (on any task, any depth) stops the WHOLE workflow after that task's result is recorded — documented semantics: from inside a loop body it does NOT mean "skip to the next iteration" or "break one level", it ends the entire run, unwinding every enclosing loop while keeping the partial per-iteration records already produced in the context. `then: continue` (or no then) means proceed normally. The run's status is 'completed' either way.

Done = `bash run_verify.sh` green.
